    "import numpy as np\n",
    "import pandas as pd\n",
    "import matplotlib.pyplot as plt\n",
    "%matplotlib inline\n",
    "\n",
    "pd.set_option('display.max_rows', 100)\n",
    "pd.set_option('display.max_columns', 100)\n",
    "from sklearn.datasets import make_classification\n",
    "from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split\n",
    "from sklearn.metrics import PrecisionRecallDisplay, RocCurveDisplay\n",
    "from sklearn.dummy import DummyClassifier\n",
    "from sklearn.metrics import average_precision_score\n",
    "from sklearn.metrics import precision_recall_curve\n",
    "from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score\n",
    "from sklearn.svm import LinearSVC\n",
    "from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression"
   ]
  },

   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Below is an **example** of working with built-in `sklearn` functions for plotting ROC and PR curves and saving the metrics. You may modify the pipeline in any way that is convenient for you.\n"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 11,
   "id": "84734039",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [
    {
     "name": "stdout",
     "output_type": "stream",
     "text": [
      "Random Classifier metrics\n",
      "AUC-PR: 0.5057\n",
      "AUC-ROC: 0.5000\n"
     ]
    },
    {
     "data": {
      "image/png": "",
      "text/plain": [
       "<Figure size 1100x500 with 2 Axes>"
      ]
     },
     "metadata": {},
     "output_type": "display_data"
    }
   ],
   "source": [
    "def depict_pr_roc(y_true, y_pred, classifier_name='Some Classifier', ax=None):\n",
    "    if ax is None:\n",
    "        fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 5))\n",
    "\n",
    "    print(classifier_name, 'metrics')\n",
    "    PrecisionRecallDisplay.from_predictions(y_true, y_pred, ax=ax[0], name=classifier_name)\n",
    "    print('AUC-PR: %.4f' % average_precision_score(y_true, y_pred))\n",
    "    ax[0].set_title(\"PRC\")\n",
    "    ax[0].set_ylim(0, 1.1)\n",
    "\n",
    "    RocCurveDisplay.from_predictions(y_true, y_pred, ax=ax[1], name=classifier_name)\n",
    "    print('AUC-ROC: %.4f' % roc_auc_score(y_true, y_pred))\n",
    "    ax[1].set_title(\"ROC\")\n",
    "    ax[1].set_ylim(0, 1.1)\n",
    "\n",
    "    plt.tight_layout()\n",
    "    plt.legend()\n",
    "\n",
    "depict_pr_roc(y_test, y_random, 'Random Classifier')"
   ]
  },

   "source": [
    "#### **Task 1.2. Visualization in SVM and LR approaches** (0.5 points)\n",
    "\n",
    "The name “support vector machine” refers to certain “support vectors.” Essentially, these are training examples that determine the position of the separating hyperplane.\n",
    "\n",
    "* Generate synthetic data using `make_classification` **with 2 features**, and train a support vector machine on it. Do not forget to fix the seed for reproducibility.\n",
    "\n",
    "* Visualize the separating line, all data points, and highlight the support vectors. Templates are provided below; you may use them or write your own."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "id": "a50c477a",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "def plot_svm_2D(X, y, model,  plot_support=True):\n",
    "\n",
    "    # create a grid\n",
    "    xx = np.linspace(X[:,0].min(), X[:,0].max(), 30)\n",
    "    yy = np.linspace(X[:,1].min(), X[:,1].max(), 30)\n",
    "    YY, XX = np.meshgrid(yy, xx)\n",
    "    xy = np.vstack([XX.ravel(), YY.ravel()]).T\n",
    "\n",
    "    # Model outputs on the grid for plotting the separating line\n",
    "    Z = # your code here\n",
    "\n",
    "    plt.contour(XX, YY, Z, colors='k', levels=[-1, 0, 1], alpha=0.5, linestyles=['--', '-', '--'])\n",
    "\n",
    "    # Plot the sample\n",
    "    plt.scatter(\n",
    "        # your code here\n",
    "    )\n",
    "\n",
    "    # Plot the support vectors\n",
    "    if plot_support:\n",
    "        plt.scatter(\n",
    "            # your code here\n",
    "            label='support vectors',\n",
    "            s=100,\n",
    "            linewidth=1,\n",
    "            edgecolor=\"blue\",\n",
    "            facecolors='none'\n",
    "        )\n",
    "\n",
    "    plt.legend()\n",
    "\n",
    "plot_svm_2D(X, y, model)"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "82577d02",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [

    "Unlike support vector machines, logistic regression does not try to construct a separating hyperplane with the maximum margin. Instead, it approximates at each point in the feature space the probability of a positive outcome, $p(y=+1|x)$. Try to plot this distribution on the plane, and do not forget to mark all the objects on it."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "id": "d231ce64",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "def plot_logreg_2D(X, y, model):\n",
    "\n",
    "    # create a grid\n",
    "    xx = np.linspace(X[:,0].min(), X[:,0].max(), 100)\n",
    "    yy = np.linspace(X[:,1].min(), X[:,1].max(), 100)\n",
    "    YY, XX = np.meshgrid(yy, xx)\n",
    "    xy = np.vstack([XX.ravel(), YY.ravel()]).T\n",
    "\n",
    "    # Model outputs on the grid for plotting the distribution\n",
    "    Z = # your code here\n",
    "    Z = Z.reshape((xx.shape[0], -1)).T\n",
    "\n",
    "    image = plt.imshow(\n",
    "        Z,\n",
    "        interpolation='nearest',\n",
    "        extent=(xx.min(), xx.max(), yy.min(), yy.max()),\n",
    "        aspect='auto',\n",
    "        origin='lower',\n",
    "        cmap=plt.cm.PuOr_r\n",
    "    )\n",
    "\n",
    "    # Plot the sample\n",
    "    plt.scatter(\n",
    "        # your code here\n",
    "        cmap=plt.cm.Paired\n",
    "    )\n",
    "\n",
    "    plt.colorbar(image)\n",
    "\n",
    "plot_logreg_2D(X, y, model)"

   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "038290bd",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "#### **Task 2. Probability Calibration** (1 point)\n",
    "\n",
    "Let us now move on to evaluating the quality of the probabilities produced by algorithms. We begin with calibration curves.\n",
    "\n",
    "Suppose an algorithm returns some numbers between zero and one. Do they estimate probability well?\n",
    "\n",
    "A well-calibrated classifier should output values such that, among the samples for which it predicts a value close to $\\alpha$, approximately $\\alpha \\cdot 100%$ actually belong to the positive class. (For example, if the classifier outputs 0.3 for some samples, then about 30% of them should belong to class 1.)\n",
    "\n",
    "To construct a calibration curve, use the following procedure:\n",
    "\n",
    "Generate synthetic data similarly to the very first task. Plot calibration curves on the test set for logistic regression and support vector machines (do not forget to transform its predictions to $[0,1]$).\n",
    "\n",
    "Plot the calibration curve of a perfectly calibrated model as well (the diagonal).\n",
    "\n",
    "Split the interval $[0, 1]$ into several small intervals of equal length.\n",
    "\n",
    "Consider the $i$-th interval with boundaries $[a_i, b_i]$ and the predictions $p_1, p_2, \\dots, p_k$ that fall into it. Let the corresponding true labels be $y_1, y_2, \\dots, y_k$. If the algorithm outputs correct probabilities, then among these true labels there should be approximately $(a_i + b_i)/2$ ones. In other words, if we draw a curve where the x-axis represents the interval centers and the y-axis represents the fractions of positive labels in those intervals, then the curve should lie along the diagonal.\n",
    "\n",
    "Below is a function that is supposed to plot such curves. It contains two mistakes — find and correct them.\n"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "id": "e6d670c1",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "def plot_calibration_curve(y_test, preds):\n",
    "    bin_middle_points = []\n",
    "    bin_real_ratios = []\n",
    "    n_bins = 10\n",
    "    for i in range(n_bins):\n",
    "        l = 1.0 / n_bins * i\n",
    "        r = 1.0 / n_bins * (i + 1)\n",
    "        bin_middle_points.append((l - r) / 2)\n",
    "        bin_real_ratios.append(np.min(y_test[(preds >= l) & (preds < r)] == 1))\n",
    "    plt.figure(figsize=(6,6))\n",
    "    plt.plot(bin_middle_points, bin_real_ratios)\n",
    "    plt.ylim([-0.05, 1.05])\n",
    "    plt.grid()"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "fde5481a",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Generate synthetic data in the same way as in the very first task. Plot calibration curves on the test set for logistic regression and support vector machines (do not forget to transform the SVM predictions to $[0,1]$). Also plot the calibration curve of a perfectly calibrated model (the diagonal)."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "id": "cc103562",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "# your code"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "dfa935a8",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "**Question:** Are the curves for SVM and logistic regression well calibrated? Think about how this follows from the shape of the curve.\n",
    "\n",
    "**Answer:** `# your answer here`"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "id": "9561d1a2",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": []
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "de185f85",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Among the formal ways to verify this are the familiar LogLoss, which directly evaluates probabilities,\n",
    "$$\\text{LogLoss} = -\\frac{1}{N}\\sum_{i} \\sum_{k \\in {0, 1}}\\log p_k[y_i = k]$$\n",
    "as well as the Brier Score, which measures the deviation between the predicted probability and the true target value.\n",
    "$$\\text{BrierScore} = \\frac{1}{N}\\sum_{i} (p_i - y_i)^2$$"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 19,
   "id": "ffdd8e5c",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "# your code here"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "4fe85a37",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [

    "\n",

  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "e73a8bd5",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "# Part 2. Processing Categorical Variables (4 points + 1.5 bonus)\n",
    "\n",
    "As we know, encoding a categorical variable as a list of numbers (for example, 1, 2, 3, ..., n) is a bad idea, because this imposes an ordering on its values that usually has no meaning.\n",
    "\n",
    "In this part, we will consider two main approaches to handling categorical values:\n",
    "\n",
    "* One-hot encoding\n",
    "* Counters (CTR, mean-target encoding, etc.) — each categorical feature is replaced by the mean value of the target variable over all objects that have the same value of this feature.\n",
    "\n",
    "Let us begin with one-hot encoding. Suppose our categorical feature $f_j(x)$ takes values from the set $C = {c_1, \\dots, c_m}$. We replace it with $m$ binary features $b_1(x), \\dots, b_m(x)$, each of which is an indicator of one of the possible category values:\n",
    "$$\n",
    "b_i(x) = [f_j(x) = c_i]\n",
    "$$\n",
    "\n",
    "#### **Data preparation**\n",
    "\n",
    "*(a priceless step)* \n",
    "\n",
    "Let us examine the [bike buyers dataset](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/heeraldedhia/bike-buyers/): customer features are given, and the task is to predict whether the customer will buy a bicycle.\n",
    "\n",
    "Replace missing values in categorical variables with a new category (`'undefined'`).\n",
    "\n",
    "Split the features into two tables: categorical features and numerical features."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "id": "3cba2b4d",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "# Read the dataset\n",
    "\n",
    "# your code here\n",
    "\n",
    "# Replace missing values in categorical variables\n",
    "\n",
    "# your code here\n",
    "\n",
    "# Separate X and y\n",
    "\n",
    "# your code here\n",
    "\n",
    "# Split into categorical and numerical features\n",
    "\n",
    "X_numerical = # your code here\n",
    "X_categorical = # your code here"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "679ee70b",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "At first, we will work only with the categorical features."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "id": "496e74c4",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(\n",
    "    X_categorical, y, test_size=0.25, random_state=777, stratify=y\n",
    ")"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "45fb4ed1",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "#### **Task 3. OrdinalEncoder** (0.5 points)\n",
    "\n",
    "Encode the categorical features using `OrdinalEncoder`. Compute the model quality (in this task we will use **`AUC-PR`**) when applying logistic regression. Measure the time required to train the model, including the feature encoding step."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "id": "6fe76817",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "# your code here"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "2c249052",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "#### **Task 4. One-Hot Encoding** (0.5 points)\n",
    "\n",
    "Encode all categorical features using one-hot encoding. Train logistic regression and see how the model quality changes (compared with what it was before encoding). Measure the time required for encoding the categorical features and training the model.\n",
    "\n",
    "As we can see, one-hot encoding can greatly increase the number of features. This affects the amount of memory required, especially if some feature has a large number of distinct values."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "id": "16ea50b0",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "# your code here"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "8ee613e4",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "#### **Task 5. Mean-target Encoding** (1 point)\n",
    "\n",
    "> The problem of feature-space explosion can be avoided with another method of encoding categorical features — mean-target encoding (for simplicity, we will refer to it as **counters**). Let us compare the effectiveness of the methods within our marketing task.\n",
    "\n",
    "> The main idea is that what matters is not the categories themselves, but the target values of the objects belonging to those categories. We replace each categorical feature with the mean value of the target variable over all objects having the same category value:\n",
    "\n",
    "$$\n",
    "g_j(x, X) = \\frac{\\sum_{i=1}^{\\ell} [f_j(x) = f_j(x_i)][y_i = +1]}{\\sum_{i=1}^{\\ell} [f_j(x) = f_j(x_i)]}\n",
    "$$\n",
    "\n",
    "Encode the categorical variables using counters exactly as described above, without any additional tricks. Train logistic regression and evaluate the model quality on the test set.\n",
    "\n",
    "Compare the training time with the previous experiments, including the encoding step."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "id": "02a940a3",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "# your code"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "5e36b00d",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "##### **Bonus: Efficient implementation** (1 point)\n",
    "\n",
    "From this point on, implement the computation of counters using a transformer inherited from `BaseEstimator` and `TransformerMixin` from `sklearn.base`.\n",
    "\n",
    "Note that:\n",
    "\n",
    "* all computations must be vectorized,\n",
    "* the transformer must not modify the input dataset in place,\n",
    "* all required statistics must be computed only on the training set inside the `fit` method.\n",
    "\n",
    "Your transformer should accept, at initialization, a list of categorical features and should transform only those features."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "id": "2e1aa032",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [

  },

   "source": [
    "##### **Bonus: another approach** (0.5 points)\n",
    "\n",
    "Compute correct counters using the first or second method described above, and do not forget to add noise as well."
   ]
  },

  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "cb283b77",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "#### **Task 7. Smoothing the counters** (1 point)\n",
    "\n",
    "Now let us answer the following question: what happens if a certain category appears only a few times in the sample? This is why smoothing of counters is used. For example, in practice, good results are often obtained by smoothing with the global mean over the whole sample:\n",
    "$$ g_j(x, X) = \\frac{\\sum_{i=1}^{\\ell} [f_j(x) = f_j(x_i)][y_i = +1] + C \\times \\text{global\\_mean}}{\\sum_{i=1}^{\\ell} [f_j(x) = f_j(x_i)] + C} $$\n",
    "\n",
    "where $\\text{global\\_mean}$ is the proportion of positive-class objects in the sample, and $C$ is a parameter controlling the degree of smoothing (you may use 10 or tune a separate value for each feature). The idea is that we “dilute” the category mean with the global mean. The larger the number of objects in the category, the weaker this effect becomes.\n",
    "\n",
    "Instead of the target mean, any other value between 0 and 1 can be used for smoothing (this parameter is sometimes called the $prior$). One can even create several features with different values of this parameter. In practice, in binary classification tasks, even negative values may sometimes be useful.\n",
    "\n",
    "Add the smoothing described above and repeat the experiments. Tune $C$ so that the quality is better than when using One-Hot Encoding."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "id": "a093bb9f",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "# your code here"
   ]
  },

The feature set includes various characteristics of a person, such as education, occupation, marital status, and so on. The target variable is binary: whether annual income exceeds 50K dollars or not.\n"

  {

   "id": "7e7ec628",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "\n",
    "An important part of the model-building process is feature selection. In practice, many features have little influence on the model, while still taking time to compute, or may even negatively affect model quality. Let us try several feature selection approaches, evaluate how they affect model quality, and measure how much time they take.\n",
    "\n",

  {

   "id": "1b13bbb5",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [

  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "90a90556",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [

  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "5a786cc7",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Suppose we want to keep only the 40 best features.\n",
    "\n",
    "Note that we must not evaluate feature quality on the test set, otherwise we may overfit, just as in hyperparameter tuning. Split the training set into 2 parts, and use one of them for validation. The original test set should be used only for the final evaluation after the filtering procedure.\n"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",

   "id": "cabc95b3",
   "metadata": {},

   "source": [
    "# your code here"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "8852b823",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Let us try to do this in the following ways:"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "69d50b5a",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "#### **Task 9. Embedded methods** (0.5 points)\n",
    "\n",
    "Let us begin with feature selection using the model itself. Different algorithms have different built-in ways of assessing feature contribution to prediction. As is well known, for a linear model this is determined by the weights, so their absolute values can be interpreted as feature importance. Such a method of feature selection is called an embedded method, since it is built into the model itself.\n",
    "\n",
    "Keep the 40 features with the largest absolute values of the corresponding linear-model coefficients. Retrain the model and evaluate its quality. Measure the speed of this feature-selection procedure.\n"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "id": "df339020",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "# your code here"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "9f840903",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Did the quality change? How?\n",
    "\n",
    "Now let us think about what we did not take into account. We assumed that features contribute on the same scale and ignored their magnitudes. If we multiply one feature by 100, then without considering regularization its weight will decrease by the same factor of 100. But we are selecting features based on that weight. Therefore, let us first scale the features using one of the methods, and only then remove the less important ones."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "id": "d0c53d32",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "# your code here"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "9a3da4ce",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "A trick question: one-hot encoding gives us binary indicator features. Try scaling them as well, just like ordinary numerical ones, and again select the top 40 features by contribution. Did the list of selected features change? Will the quality change?\n",
    "\n",
    "Keep in mind that not all scaling methods are equally good, especially in the presence of outliers.\n",
    "\n",
    "In fact, in this case you should also recompute the quality using all features as well, and do this below. If you scaled the features from the very beginning, then try selecting features on the unscaled data.\n",
    "\n",
    "What did you get?"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "id": "1fb84417",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "# your code here"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "1c8dbf05",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "#### **Task 10. Filter Methods** (0.5 points)\n",
    "\n",
    "Let us try to select features more intelligently, namely by computing some function for each feature. Based on the values of this function, we will keep the most important features. Methods of this family are called filter methods.\n",
    "\n",
    "One of the simplest functions is the correlation between a feature and the target variable. Think about the relationship between correlation and the predictive ability of the model, and how you would use information about correlation for feature selection.\n",
    "\n",
    "**Answer:** `# your code here`"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "35ab87e9",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Compute the correlation of each feature with the target and filter 40 features based on the principle you described. Then measure the quality and the selection time."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "id": "49794a8c",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "# your code here"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "7622fe1d",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Another possible function is the t-statistic:\n",
    "\n",
    "$$\n",
    "t(j) = \\frac{|\\mu_+ - \\mu_-|}{\\sqrt{\\frac{n_+ \\sigma_+^2 + n_- \\sigma_-^2}{n_+ + n_-}}}\n",
    "$$\n",
    "\n",
    "where $\\mu$, $\\sigma$, and $n$ are respectively the mean, standard deviation, and number of objects in each class.\n",
    "\n",
    "Keep the 40 features with the largest values of $t$, and measure the quality and the speed of feature selection."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "id": "8746d6b5",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "# your code here"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "66319188",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "#### **Task 11. Wrapper Methods** (1 point)\n",
    "\n",
    "The third method we consider works as follows: we remove features one by one and observe how this affects model quality. Features are eliminated greedily until some stopping condition is met, such as reaching a desired number of features or a drop in performance. More specifically, the algorithm is as follows:\n",
    "\n",
    "* $k$ is the number of features we want to keep\n",
    "* $m$ is the number of features removed at each iteration, that is, the step size\n",
    "\n",
    "Step $i$:\n",
    "\n",
    "* $F_i$ is the set of features (equal to the full set when $i=0$)\n",
    "* $M_i$ is the number of features in it, in general $\\max(k, M_{i-1} - m)$\n",
    "\n",
    "1. If exactly $k$ features remain, or if the metric decreases by more than $\\epsilon$, stop. (Not our case here, though this is also possible.)\n",
    "2. Train a model $a_i$ on the feature set $F_i$, then evaluate feature importance using any of the methods above or any other suitable approach.\n",
    "3. Select the $\\min(M_i - k, m)$ least useful features according to step 2, remove them, and move to the next step. That is, remove $m$ features if possible; otherwise remove only enough to leave exactly $k$.\n",
    "\n",
    "It is worth noting that this method can also be used in the opposite direction. Try *adding* the most useful features to the dataset until the total reaches 40. Is there a threshold after which adding further features only worsens model quality?"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "id": "b1b78957",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "# your code here"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "d676a112",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Again, keep only 40 features and evaluate the quality on the test set. Choose the step size based on some reasoning, and measure the runtime of the method."
   ]
  },

   "source": [
    "# Part 4. Evaluation of the Economic Effect of the Model (2 points)\n",
    "\n",
    "\n",
    "\n",
    "In this part, we will do something that is much closer to what would actually be required of you in real work. Namely:\n",
    "\n",
    "* we will assemble several specialized quality metrics,\n",
    "* try to tune the model to maximize *profit*,\n",
    "* and estimate how much money can actually be earned.\n",
    "\n",
    "Of course, many simplifying assumptions will be made here, but let us proceed step by step. If you missed your economics classes, recall that revenue is the amount of money brought in by customers, while profit is revenue minus costs such as salaries and other expenses."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "b029bcb3",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "#### **Task 12. Forecasting Income and Expenses** (1 point)\n",
    "\n",
    "In this part, we will work with the [UCI Bank Marketing Dataset](https://archive.ics.uci.edu/ml/datasets/bank+marketing). This dataset contains information on telephone marketing campaigns conducted by a bank.\n",
    "\n",
    "The **object** here is a phone call to a potential client offering some service, said to be a short-term deposit. The feature description includes client characteristics, such as education and marital status, call-related information, and various economic indicators. More detailed information is available in the file `bank-additional-names.txt`.\n",
    "\n",
    "The **target variable** is the client’s response, that is, whether the client agreed to open a deposit."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "id": "2a682cdf",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "!wget https://archive.ics.uci.edu/ml/machine-learning-databases/00222/bank-additional.zip\n",
    "!unzip bank-additional.zip\n",
    "df = pd.read_csv('bank-additional/bank-additional-full.csv', sep=';')\n",
    "\n",
    "df.head()\n",
    "\n",
    "X = df.drop(columns=['duration', 'y'])\n",
    "y = (df.y == 'yes')"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "0c21d590",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "In this part, there is no need to split the dataset in advance — we will use cross-validation.\n",
    "\n",
    "Use the parameters that you consider most appropriate (`shuffle`, `stratify`, number of folds, etc.). Cross-validation will produce several versions of the training and test sets. For convenience, you may use the template below, which will gradually gain functionality as you work through the assignment. As usual, this is optional, but it is highly desirable to save the results of your experiments, since we will compare them at the end."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "id": "cf616db3",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "from collections import defaultdict\n",
    "from sklearn.model_selection import KFold\n",
    "\n",
    "def cross_validate(\n",
    "    X,\n",
    "    y,\n",
    "    n_splits=5,\n",
    "    random_state=None,\n",
    "    shuffle=False,\n",
    "    # other arguments that may be useful later\n",
    "):\n",
    "    metrics = []\n",
    "    # or any other fold splitter, see model_selection\n",
    "    kf = KFold(n_splits=n_splits, random_state=random_state, shuffle=shuffle)\n",
    "\n",
    "    for train_index, test_index in kf.split(X):\n",
    "\n",
    "        # take the dataset and train the model\n",
    "        # your code here\n",
    "\n",
    "        # compute the metrics you need and add the results from each fold\n",
    "        metric_dict = {\n",
    "            # \"metric_key\": metric_value\n",
    "        }\n",
    "        metrics.append(metric_dict)\n",
    "\n",
    "    # all that remains is to wrap everything nicely\n",
    "    return pd.DataFrame(metrics)"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "08db3693",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Choose a classification metric that seems appropriate to you, and train logistic regression on each training fold. Encode categorical features using the method you liked best above, normalize numerical features, keep hyperparameters at their default values, make predictions for the corresponding test folds, and print the results."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "id": "ef3eccb6",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "# your code here"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "7e776fdd",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Suppose that your call-center operators are paid 2 dollars for each client they call. If the client agrees to the proposed conditions, that client brings the bank 10 dollars. Assume that your employees decide to call all clients with positive predictions.\n",
    "\n",
    "In our task, we will use profit as the business metric, and accordingly we will choose the best model based on this metric.\n",
    "\n",
    "Compute the revenue on all test folds and save the results for the business metric together with the classification metric you selected earlier.\n",
    "\n",
    "Answer the following questions:\n",
    "\n",
    "* How much money will you earn on average?\n",
    "* What is the standard deviation of profit?\n",
    "* How much of the earned money will have to be paid to the call-center operators?\n",
    "* Is the business metric proportional to the selected classification metric?"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "id": "3cb09054",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "# your code here"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "6b91798f",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "\n",
    "Now let us introduce some randomness. Suppose that a client who agrees to the conditions brings not 10 dollars, but a random amount uniformly distributed on the interval $[0, 20)$. Repeat all the same steps. To imitate a real situation, do **not** fix the random seed when computing the revenue from a client, though of course keep it fixed for the fold splits. What happened?"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": null,
   "id": "f66f2fb3",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "# your code here"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "e3af6503",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [

   "source": [
    "#### **Task 15. Softmax Regression** (1 point) \n",
    "\n",
    "However, curious machine learners may ask: “Why do we need all this stuff if the sigmoid has a generalization for the multiclass case?” If you enjoyed computing gradients in the previous homework, or if you like training neural networks, this part is for you. Here we will try to build a single model that predicts everything, and also compare it with the variants above. \n",
    "\n",
    "Let us begin by computing the loss. Recall that the logistic loss is a special case of cross-entropy, and that is what we will try to optimize. \n",
    "\n",
    "$$\n",
    "\\text{CE}(X, y) = -\\frac{1}{N}\\sum_i \\sum_k [y_i = k] \\log p(x_i = k)\n",
    "$$\n",
    "\n",
    "In this case, we will compute probabilities using softmax, which is the general case of the sigmoid:\n",
    "\n",
    "$$\n",
    "p(x_i) = \\text{Softmax}(a(x_i)); \\quad\n",
    "\\text{Softmax}(x)_k = \\frac{e^{x_k}}{\\sum_j e^{x_j}}\n",
    "$$\n",
    "\n",
    "\n",
    "The prediction of the model for one object will now be made using a weight matrix, since there are multiple outputs:\n",
    "\n",
    "$$\n",
    "a(x_i) = x_i \\cdot W\n",
    "$$"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "88e024bc",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [

  },

   "execution_count": null,
   "id": "0e9f4ef3",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "from typing import Iterable, Optional\n",

    "import torch\n",

    "    y_true: np.ndarray[int],\n",
    ") -> float:\n",
    "    # your code here\n",
    "    return\n",
    "\n",
    "for _ in range(1000):\n",
    "\n",
    "    n_objects = np.random.randint(1, 100)\n",
    "    n_classes = np.random.randint(2, 20)\n",
    "    y_pred = np.random.normal(0, 1, (n_objects, n_classes))\n",
    "    y_true = np.random.randint(low=0, high=n_classes, size=(n_objects,))\n",
    "\n",
    "    your_ce = custom_ce(y_pred, y_true)  # do not forget to adjust if you changed the template\n",
    "    torch_ce = cross_entropy(torch.tensor(y_pred), torch.tensor(y_true))\n",
    "    assert np.allclose(your_ce, torch_ce), \"Something went wrong\""
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "b6c0120f",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [

    "\n",
    "* in matrix form, the derivative is not easy to obtain; first try doing it for one object, then generalizing will be easier\n",
    "* `logsoftmax` is much easier to differentiate than plain `softmax`\n",
    "* do not forget the chain rule\n",
    "* since the weights are a matrix here, the result will also be a matrix; keep this in mind when checking dimensions\n",
    "* if you have not yet figured out how to convert indicators into vector form, now is the time"
   ]
  },
